title: Grain Delay
summary: Granular processor — chops audio into tiny grains and plays them back with random pitch/position variation. Creates textures and clouds from any audio source. Not a traditional delay — a texture device.
steps: Add Grain Delay to an atmospheric pad or texture track
Spray: how random the grain position is. Low spray (0–20%) = coherent granular shimmer. High spray (60–100%) = pitch clouds, the source becomes unrecognisable
Frequency: number of grains per second. High frequency = smooth, dense texture. Low frequency = stuttery, fragmented sound
Pitch: amount of random pitch variation applied to grains. 0 = no pitch randomness. High values = tonal clouds that spread harmonically
Delay Time: base delay before grains play back. Set low (10–50ms) for near-instant texture. Longer for a distinct delay tail
Dry/Wet: 30–50% blends the texture with the original. 100% = only the granular output (original signal removed)
tips: Grain Delay is most effective on atmospheric or pad sounds — drums and bass usually sound unpleasant when granulated
For a breakdown 'dissolve' effect: automate Spray from 0% to 80% over 8 bars as the breakdown fades in. The pad becomes a texture cloud
Automation of the Pitch knob over time creates an evolving harmonic shimmer — the textural equivalent of a very slow filter sweep
Grain Delay + Reverb: granulate a pad and send it heavily to reverb. The result is a complex, evolving atmospheric texture perfect for breakdowns
practice_hint: In a session with a chord pad, add Grain Delay. Start with Spray 20%, Frequency 80, Dry/Wet 40%. Loop 4 bars. Then gradually increase Spray to 80% and listen to the pad dissolve into texture.
ableton_version_note: Available in all editions of Live 9+.
